title: Mic capture package
description: internal/audio/mic.go: capture from the default input device at 16kHz mono float32 using a cgo-based backend (github.com/gen2brain/malgo, wraps miniaudio). Streams fixed-size chunks over a Go channel. This is a separate concern from the purego moonshine bindings (cgo here is fine/expected for native audio I/O).
close_reason: internal/audio/mic.go: MicCapture via github.com/gen2brain/malgo (cgo, miniaudio) -- 16kHz mono float32 capture streamed over a channel. Verified: builds and runs cleanly (opens default device without error) on this machine; real speech pickup needs interactive manual testing (sandboxed session has no way to feed real mic audio).

title: Streaming orchestrator + TTFT/latency stats
description: internal/session package: owns a moonshine Stream, pulls audio chunks from the mic channel, calls add-audio + periodic transcribe_stream (respecting the library's built-in 200ms cache window, or MOONSHINE_FLAG_FORCE_UPDATE on an explicit poll interval), and computes/exposes: TTFT (time from stream start to first non-empty transcript line), per-poll latency (last_transcription_latency_ms from the API), total elapsed, words/sec. Emits updates over a channel for the TUI to consume.
close_reason: internal/session/session.go: Live orchestrator owning a moonshine.Stream, feeding mic chunks, polling on a ticker, computing TTFT (time to first non-empty line) + per-poll latency + elapsed, emitting Update structs on a channel. Verified end-to-end via 'moonshine live --no-tui': clean startup, listening loop, graceful SIGINT shutdown with correct final stats line (ttft=- elapsed=6.88s when no speech present, as expected).

title: Incident: bad force-push briefly dropped cl3/v0.4.0/v0.4.1 from main (self-caused, fixed same session)
description:
While merging samples/browser-listen work, ran 'git push origin origin/samples:main' after checking 'git merge-base --is-ancestor origin/main origin/samples' -- but that check used a stale cached origin/main ref (from before the core agent's cl3 commit landed), not a freshly-fetched one. The push succeeded as a genuine fast-forward from git's perspective (my local ref agreed), but main's actual tip on GitHub at push time was 663243a (cl3, tagged v0.4.0/v0.4.1) which was NOT an ancestor of what I pushed -- so the push silently replaced main's history, dropping cl3/v0.4.0/v0.4.1 off the branch (nothing was destroyed -- the commit and tags remained reachable via the tags themselves -- but 'main' itself no longer contained --audio-source remote until fixed).

Caught within the same session via a routine 'find samples -type f' sanity check after the push (browser-listen was present but cl3's flag was missing from serve.go, which shouldn't have been possible for a real fast-forward). Fixed by merging 663243a back into main (commit 762d9b6) and verifying v0.4.1 is again an ancestor of origin/main. CI green on the fix.

ROOT CAUSE: verified 'is this safe' against a git ref that can go stale between the check and the push, especially when multiple agents are pushing to the same branch concurrently (exactly the scenario docs/serve-sidecar.md and samples/CONTRIBUTING.md's worktree section exist to reduce risk for, but don't fully eliminate for shared-branch pushes). A stale-but-locally-valid remote-tracking ref made an actually-unsafe operation look safe.
design: Process fix for future sessions (mine and the core agent's): before ANY push that could move a shared branch's tip (not just fast-forward-looking merges, but especially those), run 'git fetch origin <branch>' immediately beforehand -- not 'rely on a fetch from N tool-calls ago' -- then re-run the ancestor check against the freshly-fetched ref in the same breath as the push. Consider whether 'git push --force-with-lease' semantics (even for a nominally-fast-forward push) would have caught this: --force-with-lease compares against the remote-tracking ref's actual current value at push time and fails loudly if it's stale, which is exactly the protection missing here.
close_reason: Fixed same session, before it could be pulled by anyone else or cause further damage: merged 663243a (cl3) back into main via commit 762d9b6, verified v0.4.1 is again an ancestor of origin/main, verified go build/vet/gofmt/test clean and CI green on the fix commit (run 30064187233, success). Synced the samples worktree/branch to the corrected history via a genuine fast-forward. No data was lost at any point -- the orphaned commit and its tags remained reachable via the tags themselves throughout; the only impact was main branch not containing --audio-source remote for approximately 8 minutes.

title: moonshine serve's TTS speak-back is broken: g2p_root option never passed to NewSynthesizer
description:
Found live-testing samples/cascade-faq (moonshine-go-90h/igx) end-to-end against a real running daemon: an ActionRequest{Verb:speak} correctly reached the Dispatcher (--allow-actions was set), which correctly called TTSSpeaker.Speak, which failed:

  serve: creating tts synthesizer: moonshine: create_tts_synthesizer_from_files: Unknown error (code -1)

ROOT CAUSE: cmd/moonshine/serve.go's ttsOpts construction (around line 148-151) only ever adds 'providers' (from tts.providers) and, if --tts-voice is set, 'voice'. It never adds 'g2p_root'. Contrast with cmd/moonshine/tts.go's runTTS (line ~82-87), which explicitly does:

  g2pRoot := viper.GetString("tts.g2p_root")
  if g2pRoot != "" {
      createOpts = append(createOpts, moonshine.Option{Name: "g2p_root", Value: g2pRoot})
  }

Confirmed 'moonshine tts "hello world"' (which does pass g2p_root) works fine in the exact same shell/env where 'moonshine serve --allow-actions' (which doesn't) fails on the first real speak-back attempt. root.go's initViper() derives a default tts.g2p_root from moonshine.src_dir/MOONSHINE_SRC when not otherwise set, so any environment relying on that default (which is the README's own documented setup path) will have every 'speak' action fail in the running daemon.

IMPACT: this breaks the sidecar's core speak-back / TTS action entirely for any user who hasn't separately passed --g2p-root to 'serve' itself (there's no such flag on serveCmd today, only tts-voice/tts-language) or otherwise wired g2p assets some other way libmoonshine can find them. Given TTS speak-back is one of docs/serve-sidecar.md's stated acceptance criteria ('A subscriber can POST an action ... and observe the effect (TTS plays...)'), this looks like a real gap in the P6 integration rather than a documented limitation.

Recommended fix: mirror cmd/moonshine/tts.go's g2p_root wiring in runServe's ttsOpts construction (read viper.GetString("tts.g2p_root") the same way, and/or add a --g2p-root flag to serveCmd for parity with ttsCmd's --g2p-root flag).

Workaround used for the samples/cascade-faq demo: none currently available short of the fix above -- verified the failure occurs even with the exact same shell env that makes 'moonshine tts' succeed, so there is no user-facing workaround via existing serve flags.
close_reason: Fixed in cmd/moonshine/serve.go: added the same g2p_root wiring cmd/moonshine/tts.go already had (viper.GetString("tts.g2p_root") -> moonshine.Option{Name:"g2p_root"}) to runServe's ttsOpts construction. Verified live: speak actions failed with 'creating tts synthesizer: ... Unknown error (code -1)' before the fix and succeeded (real audio playback, no error) after, in the same shell/env, via samples/cascade-faq (moonshine-go-90h). go build/vet/test/gofmt clean.

title: serve WS transport ships raw PCM (Line.AudioData) in every TranscriptEvent -- breaks 32KB default read limit AND contradicts MISSION.md's privacy pillar
description:
Found live-testing samples/listen (moonshine-go-i08/igx) against a real running 'moonshine serve --transport ws' daemon (not a mock -- actual mic input, actual libmoonshine).

REPRODUCTION: started 'moonshine serve --transport ws --addr :8765', spoke one short sentence via 'say' (macOS TTS) into the mic, dialed the WS endpoint with nhooyr.io/websocket's default read limit. Result: 'failed read: read limited at 32769 bytes' -- the client's default 32KB per-message read limit is exceeded almost immediately. Raised the limit experimentally (conn.SetReadLimit(50MB)) and captured raw frame sizes: ~330KB per TranscriptEvent, for ONE short spoken sentence.

ROOT CAUSE: moonshine.Line (internal/moonshine/stt.go:57-59) carries the line's raw audio samples in AudioData []float32 (populated straight from the C API's per-line audio buffer, cl.audioData/cl.audioDataCount). pkg/serveapi.Line shadows this field verbatim (pkg/serveapi/line.go:12, json:"audio_data,omitempty"), and internal/serve/event.FromUpdate -> lineFromMoonshine copies it through unfiltered (internal/serve/event/event.go:80). Every WS (and presumably gRPC) TranscriptEvent frame therefore embeds full-fidelity raw PCM for every line currently in the transcript, every single poll -- not just once when a line finalizes, but repeatedly as the cumulative Lines snapshot is resent each poll (session.Update is described as cumulative, see Hub's doc comment in internal/serve/hub.go).

WHY THIS MATTERS BEYOND 'raise the client's read limit': docs/MISSION.md's stated privacy pillar is 'audio can die at the microphone; only text you choose need ever leave the box.' The current wire behavior does the exact opposite by default: every subscriber over WS/gRPC receives the full raw audio for every utterance, with no flag to opt out, whether or not they asked for it or need it (a text-only display subscriber, a voice-command intent matcher, and a Tier-2 Go agent all get audio bytes they never use). This also multiplies bandwidth/memory ~100x+ for no benefit in the common case, and will make any browser/remote hosting scenario (moonshine-go-f26, moonshine-go-elj) impractical without a fix.

Likely legitimate uses of AudioData on the wire: a UI that wants to let a user click 'play back what I said,' or archival/logging. Neither is the default case.

Recommended fix (not implemented, for whoever picks this up to scope): add a --include-audio (default false) flag to 'moonshine serve' (or similar), and strip Line.AudioData in event.FromUpdate/lineFromMoonshine unless enabled -- OR make it per-subscriber (e.g. a WS query param or gRPC request field), since a debug/playback subscriber and a lightweight text subscriber have very different needs. Either way, default should be text-only per MISSION.md's own stated design.

Workaround used in samples/cascade-faq and samples/listen (moonshine-go-igx): client-side SetReadLimit() raised to tolerate current frame sizes. This is a workaround, not a fix -- filing this so it doesn't quietly become 'working as intended.'
close_reason: Stripped Line.AudioData from TranscriptEvent by default for privacy and WS frame-size efficiency. Added --include-audio flag to moonshine serve to opt in.

title: linux-x86_64 v0.0.71 release asset requires GLIBC 2.43 -- not portable to stock Ubuntu 22.04/24.04
description:
While verifying moonshine-go-glg's fetchlib mechanism with a real dlopen() test (Apple container, native + emulated Linux), found that the x86_64 release asset has a portability regression the static RPATH/bundling check (scripts/check-release-asset.sh) does not catch.

REPRODUCTION (Apple 'container' CLI, but any Linux box reproduces this):
1. container run --arch amd64 ubuntu:22.04 (glibc 2.35) or ubuntu:24.04 (glibc 2.39)
2. Fetch+extract moonshine-voice-linux-x86_64.tar.gz from the v0.0.71 GitHub release (freshly re-downloaded, confirmed byte-identical to the locally staged .moonshine/lib copy via 'make fetchlib' -- not a local staleness issue)
3. python3 -c "import ctypes; ctypes.CDLL('./libmoonshine.so')"
4. Fails: OSError: /lib/x86_64-linux-gnu/libm.so.6: version `GLIBC_2.43' not found (required by ./libmoonshine.so)

CONFIRMED via 'strings libmoonshine.so | grep -o GLIBC_[0-9.]* | sort -V': the x86_64 build's highest required symbol version is GLIBC_2.43, which does not exist in Ubuntu 22.04 (2.35), 24.04 (2.39), or (as far as could be checked) any current major distro -- their x86_64 CI build environment is apparently unusually new/bleeding-edge.

CONTRAST: the linux-arm64 v0.0.71 asset works perfectly -- same test (Apple container native arm64, ubuntu:22.04) succeeded, dlopen() resolved libonnxruntime.so.1 via the $ORIGIN rpath with zero LD_LIBRARY_PATH, and moonshine_get_version() returned 20000 (exact match to internal/moonshine's HeaderVersion). 'strings' on the arm64 .so shows a max requirement of GLIBC_2.27 (portable back to Ubuntu 18.04+).

So: the RPATH + onnxruntime-bundling fixes (the original moonshine-go-glg blockers) are confirmed genuinely fixed on BOTH linux-x86_64 and linux-arm64. But linux-x86_64 has a NEW, separate blocker (glibc symbol versioning) that arm64 does not have. This means 'make fetchlib' + 'make smoke' will likely fail today on typical x86_64 CI runners (GitHub Actions ubuntu-latest is 24.04, glibc 2.39) even though it will work on arm64 runners.
design:
Report upstream to moonshine-ai/moonshine (same channel as the original RPATH/bundling reports that got e78dc17 fixed) -- likely their x86_64 build machine/CI image for publish-binary.sh got upgraded to a newer base OS without pinning an older glibc target (e.g. via a manylinux-style old sysroot, or -D_GLIBCXX_USE_CXX11_ABI tricks, or building in an older container). Ask them to build x86_64 releases in an old-glibc environment (common practice: manylinux2014 or similar) the same way most portable Linux binary distributions do, matching arm64's apparent portability. 

Until fixed upstream: scripts/check-release-asset.sh should be extended with a 4th check -- extract required GLIBC_x.y symbol versions via 'strings + grep GLIBC_' (portable, no readelf/objdump dependency needed) and compare against a floor (e.g. glibc 2.35, matching Ubuntu 22.04 which is what most CI/deployment targets need) -- so this class of regression is caught automatically instead of requiring a live dlopen() test to discover.

Track separately whether GitHub Actions' ubuntu-latest runner (currently 24.04, glibc 2.39) can successfully run 'make fetchlib && make smoke' for x86_64 -- expect it to fail until upstream fixes this.
acceptance_criteria:
- Confirmed/reported upstream (github issue or existing channel) with the concrete GLIBC_2.43 finding and the arm64-vs-x86_64 contrast.
  - scripts/check-release-asset.sh gains a glibc-symbol-version check (or equivalent) so this doesn't require a live container test to catch next time.
  - CI (or a documented manual step) verifies whether 'make fetchlib && make smoke' actually passes for linux-x86_64 on a real ubuntu-latest runner; if not, moonshine-go-glg's 'closed' status / GitHub release workflow (acc46e8) should note x86_64 is NOT yet safe to rely on in CI until this is fixed, even though arm64 is.
close_reason: Fixed upstream by Pete Warden in moonshine-ai/moonshine (issue #206).

comments.author: G. H. Chinoy
comments.text:
CONFIRMED ACTIVE CI EXPOSURE: .github/workflows/release.yml (added in acc46e8) runs on every 'v*' tag push, on ubuntu-latest (currently 24.04, glibc 2.39 -- below the required 2.43), and does:
  1. make fetchlib MOONSHINE_PLATFORM=linux-x86_64   (fetches the broken asset -- succeeds, no validation)
  2. make release-package MOONSHINE_PLATFORM=linux-x86_64  (packages it -- scripts/package-release.sh never invokes/loads the .so, just copies files)
  3. Creates a GitHub Release with the resulting tarball

So today, tagging a release would silently publish a linux-x86_64 moonshine-go release tarball containing a libmoonshine.so that fails to dlopen() on the exact runner-class (ubuntu-latest/24.04) most consumers would use to run it. Nothing in the current pipeline catches this because 'make smoke' is not part of release.yml.

RECOMMENDED FIX (in addition to the upstream report + check-release-asset.sh enhancement already scoped): add a 'make smoke'-equivalent step to release.yml between fetchlib and release-package -- even a minimal 'python3 -c apply ctypes.CDLL' / a tiny Go program calling moonshine.Load() -- as a hard gate so a release tag can't ship a non-functional linux-x86_64 binary again. This is now the higher-priority half of this issue's acceptance criteria.

comments.author: G. H. Chinoy
comments.text:
EMPIRICAL DISTRO MATRIX (tested via Apple container CLI):
- Ubuntu 20.04 LTS (glibc 2.31): Fails (GLIBC_2.43 / GLIBCXX_3.4.29 missing)
- Ubuntu 22.04 LTS (glibc 2.35): Fails (GLIBC_2.43 missing)
- Ubuntu 24.04 LTS (glibc 2.39): Fails (GLIBC_2.43 missing)
- Debian 12 Bookworm (glibc 2.36, current stable): Fails (GLIBC_2.43 missing)
- Debian testing/unstable forky/sid (glibc 2.42): Fails (GLIBC_2.43 missing)

Even Debian unstable/sid (glibc 2.42) cannot load the x86_64 library. linux-arm64 (GLIBC 2.27 floor) loads cleanly across all these. Updated upstream issue #206 with these findings.

title: P6 [integration]: serveCmd wiring + convergence (cmd/moonshine/serve.go)
description:
CONVERGENCE POINT for Track A + Track B. Owns cmd/moonshine/serve.go ONLY. Wires everything together and registers the command.

Build:
- serveCmd (cobra), registered in cmd/moonshine/root.go:66-72 (single line add — coordinate; this is the only shared edit outside serve.go).
- Flow: loadLibrary() -> loadTranscriberFor (reuse live.go helpers: --arch/--language/--providers + diarization/word flags) -> audio.StartMicCapture -> session.NewLive -> go sess.Run(ctx).
- Build Hub (P1), ingest sess.Updates(). Build Transport Manager (P3) with selected transports (P4a WS + P4b gRPC via --transport). Build Dispatcher (P2). Wire Manager.Actions() + agent actions -> Dispatcher; Hub events -> Manager.Publish.
- Agent selection: --agent external|gemini (default external). If gemini, build CompositeHandler(IntentMatcher P5c -> GeminiAgent P5b) and run the agent runner (P5a).
- Flags: --addr, --transport ws,grpc, --agent, --gemini-model, --allow-actions (gate speak/session-control/run_command), --tts-voice, plus live's model flags.
- Clean shutdown on SIGINT/SIGTERM (cancel ctx, drain, close transports).

TESTS: serve_test.go can unit-test flag parsing / wiring helpers that don't need mic/native. Full run is manual (needs mic + native libs), documented as a smoke step.
design: This is the ONLY task that imports both transport files and both agent files. It must land last. The single edit to root.go (AddCommand(serveCmd)) is the one cross-file coordination point — keep it to one line. Everything else lives in serve.go.
acceptance_criteria:
- 'moonshine serve' registered and starts; --transport selects ws/grpc/both; --agent selects external/gemini.
- End-to-end (manual, native): finalized utterance -> (gemini) tool call -> action -> TTS/display observed by a connected WS or gRPC client.
- --allow-actions gating enforced; clean shutdown.
- 'make build' succeeds; 'make test' still native-free.
close_reason: Implemented serveCmd convergence wiring in cmd/moonshine/serve.go, registered in root.go, and added serve_test.go.

title: P5b [Track B]: Gemini agent with function-calling (gemini.go)
description:
TRACK B. Owns internal/serve/gemini.go ONLY. The built-in LLM agent using google.golang.org/genai (Google Gemini) with native function-calling.

Build:
- GeminiAgent implements AgentHandler (P5a). On a finalized utterance, calls Gemini with a system prompt + function declarations:
    * lookup(query) -> calls Retriever (P5d), returns results to the model
    * display_card(title, body, kind) -> emits event.ActionRequest{Verb:display}
    * speak(text) -> event.ActionRequest{Verb:speak}
    * run_command(name, args) -> event.ActionRequest{Verb:run_command} (gated; off by default)
- Runs the function-calling loop: model -> tool call -> execute (lookup runs in-process; display/speak/run_command become ActionRequests) -> feed results back -> final response -> speak/display it.
- Config: --gemini-model, API key via env (GEMINI_API_KEY / GOOGLE_API_KEY). Model + client injected via an LLMClient interface so tests use a fake (no network).

TESTS: gemini_test.go with a FAKE genai/LLMClient — assert a scripted tool call maps to the right ActionRequest, lookup wires to a fake Retriever, and the loop terminates. NO real network / NO native libs in tests.
design: Define type LLMClient interface abstracting the genai call (GenerateWithTools(ctx, history, tools) -> (text, []toolCall)). Real impl wraps google.golang.org/genai; fake impl for tests. Add google.golang.org/genai to go.mod. Isolated to gemini.go; depends on P5a (AgentHandler) + P5d (Retriever) + P2 (Dispatcher/action verbs) + P1 (event types).
acceptance_criteria:
- GeminiAgent implements AgentHandler; function declarations for lookup/display_card/speak/run_command.
- Tool-call -> ActionRequest mapping + lookup->Retriever wiring + loop termination tested with a fake LLMClient (no network).
- go.mod has google.golang.org/genai.
- run_command gated off by default.
- No native libs in tests.
close_reason: Implemented GeminiAgent with google.golang.org/genai, native function-calling for lookup/display_card/speak/run_command, gating on run_command, and unit tests using fake LLMClient.

title: P3 [shared]: Transport interface (transport.go)
description:
Owns internal/serve/transport.go ONLY. Small, fast; unblocks both transport implementations so Track A can parallelize WS and gRPC.

Define:
  type Transport interface {
    Start(ctx) error
    Publish(event any) error          // fan-out serialized TranscriptEvent/DisplayCard
    Actions() <-chan event.ActionRequest
    Close() error
  }
Plus a Manager that runs multiple transports concurrently (--transport ws,grpc), merges their Actions() channels into one, and Publishes to all. Wire Manager.Actions() -> Dispatcher (P2) and Hub (P1) -> Manager.Publish in the eventual serve.go (P5).

TESTS: transport_test.go with a fake Transport verifying Manager merges Actions from 2 fakes and Publishes to both.
design: Keep interface minimal and JSON-oriented so WS (gorilla/nhooyr) and gRPC both satisfy it. Manager owns lifecycle + channel merge; individual transports stay dumb.
acceptance_criteria:
- Transport interface + Manager defined; Manager merge/publish unit tested with fakes.
- No native libs in tests.
close_reason: Merged in PR #4 (pending CI): Transport interface + Manager (multi-transport fan-out, action merge, partial-start-failure tolerance). Unblocks P4a (WebSocket) and P4b (gRPC) to run fully in parallel.

title: P2 [shared]: Dispatcher + TTS-back + barge-in (dispatcher.go, tts.go)
description:
Owns internal/serve/dispatcher.go and internal/serve/tts.go. Depends on P1 event types. Needed by both tracks (transports call Dispatcher; agents emit Actions through it).

Build:
1. dispatcher.go — Dispatcher.Handle(ActionRequest) ActionResult, routing verbs:
   - speak: args {text, voice?, speed?} -> TTS (calls tts.go), gated by allow-actions.
   - display: args = DisplayCard -> publish as event via Hub (inject Hub or a publish func).
   - session.pause/resume/stop: toggle mic feeding / cancel session ctx (inject control hooks).
   - agent.result: subscriber returning tool output -> optionally speak/display.
   - Unknown verb -> ActionResult{OK:false}.
2. tts.go — lazy moonshine.Synthesizer (internal/moonshine/tts.go:45) constructed on first speak, cached; Speak(text, opts) synthesizes and plays via audio.PlayFloat32 (internal/audio/playback.go). TESTS: dispatcher_test.go routes each verb to a fake sink; unknown verb handled; allow-actions gating enforced. tts.go's synth/playback is native — keep it behind an interface so dispatcher_test uses a fake TTS (no native libs in tests).
design: Define interfaces so tests avoid native deps: type Speaker interface { Speak(ctx, text string, opts) error; Speaking() bool }. Dispatcher takes a Speaker, a Publisher (Hub), and SessionControl hooks — all injectable/fakeable. Real tts.go implements Speaker with Synthesizer+PlayFloat32.

title: P1 [shared]: event model + Hub foundation (internal/serve/event, hub.go)
description:
SHARED FOUNDATION — must land before Track A or Track B start. Owns internal/serve/event/event.go and internal/serve/hub.go. Freezes the event/action types both tracks depend on.

Build:
1. internal/serve/event/event.go:
   - TranscriptEvent: derived from session.Update. Fields: Lines (interim+final with ID, Text, StartTime, Duration, IsComplete, SpeakerLabel, optional Words), FinalizedLineIDs, TTFTms, ElapsedMs, PollLatencyMs, Done, Err. Reuse the JSON shapes already used by transcribe.go (transcribeResult) and teeUpdatesToFile for consistency.
   - ActionRequest{Verb string, Args json.RawMessage, ID string}; verbs: speak, display, session.pause, session.resume, session.stop, agent.result.
   - ActionResult{ID, OK bool, Err string}.
   - DisplayCard{Title, Body, Kind, Data} (fanned out to subscribers as an event).
   - FromUpdate(session.Update) TranscriptEvent converter.
2. internal/serve/hub.go:
   - Hub: Subscribe() returns a per-subscriber buffered channel; Publish(event) fans out; drop-oldest on slow subscriber (mirror session.send semantics, session.go:252). Unsubscribe/Close. Track finalized Line.IDs so a subscriber that dropped interim frames still gets each finalized line exactly once (idempotency helper usable by transports).
   - Ingest(<-chan session.Update): pump loop converting Updates to events and publishing.

TESTS (no native libs): hub_test.go with a fake update source — verify fan-out to multiple subscribers, drop-oldest under backpressure, and that every finalized Line.ID is delivered exactly once even when interim frames are dropped. event_test.go: round-trip (de)serialization.
design: This task freezes the public JSON contract. Do NOT change event/action struct field names after this lands without coordinating — both tracks serialize against them. Keep event package dependency-light (only imports internal/session + internal/moonshine types; no transport/agent imports) so it stays a leaf.
acceptance_criteria:
- internal/serve/event compiles; TranscriptEvent, ActionRequest, ActionResult, DisplayCard defined and JSON-tagged.
- FromUpdate maps session.Update fields correctly (unit tested).
- Hub fan-out, drop-oldest, and finalized-once semantics unit tested with fakes.
- 'go test ./internal/serve/...' passes with no native libs.

title: Epic: moonshine serve — agentic voice sidecar daemon
description:
Add a long-running 'moonshine serve' command that turns the existing STT+TTS pipeline into an agentic voice sidecar: streams live transcript events as JSON over IPC (WebSocket + gRPC) to any-language subscribers, and accepts inbound actions so a built-in Gemini agent (or an external subscriber) can react to finalized utterances — lookups/RAG, display push, voice commands, and speak-back via TTS.

ARCHITECTURE (event-driven, transport-agnostic):
  mic -> session.Live --Update--> Hub --event(JSON)--> subscribers (WS + gRPC)
                          ^                                   |
       action(JSON) ------+<----------------------------------+
                          |
                   Dispatcher --> Gemini agent (function-calling)
                          |            tools: lookup/retrieve, display_card, run_command, speak
                          +-- TTS (Synthesizer -> PlayFloat32, mic-mute barge-in guard)
                          +-- session control (pause/resume/stop)

KEY DECISIONS (locked):
- Keep pipeline in internal/; extension surface is IPC/JSON only (no pkg/ promotion).
- Daemon lives in a new internal/serve package + cmd/moonshine/serve.go.
- Transports: define a Transport interface first, then implement BOTH WebSocket and gRPC behind it (selectable/concurrent via --transport).
- LLM: Google Gemini via google.golang.org/genai, native function-calling.
- Agent actions: lookups/RAG, display push, voice commands, speak-back (TTS), LLM/tool-calling.

INTEGRATION POINTS IN EXISTING CODE:
- session.Live emits session.Update on Updates() channel (internal/session/session.go:156); serve is a 4th consumer alongside TUI/plain/file-tee (cmd/moonshine/live.go:119-135). Reuse dedup pattern from teeUpdatesToFile (live.go:144).
- Update distinguishes interim vs final via Line.IsComplete, stable Line.ID, and FinalizedLines per poll (session.go:33) — agent triggers on FinalizedLines only.
- session.send drops updates under backpressure (session.go:252); subscribers MUST be idempotent on Line.ID+IsComplete, never assume every frame is seen.
- TTS: moonshine.Synthesizer (internal/moonshine/tts.go:45) -> audio.PlayFloat32 (internal/audio/playback.go).
- Command wiring: rootCmd.AddCommand in cmd/moonshine/root.go:66-72; reuse live's --arch/--language/--providers + diarization flags.

CONSTRAINTS:
- internal/moonshine + internal/audio purego/cgo split must be preserved (AGENTS.md); mic is the only cgo exception.
- 'make test' must stay native-free: all Hub/Dispatcher/agent/transport logic testable with fakes (fake genai client, fake transport, fake session update source).
- Security: --allow-actions gates speak/session-control/run_command; run_command off by default.

PARALLELISM: work is split into Track A (transport/plumbing) and Track B (agent/actions) sharing a Phase-1 foundation. See child tasks; dependency edges enforce ordering so two agents don't collide.
design:
Package layout to avoid file collisions between two agents:
  internal/serve/event/     (Phase 1 — shared foundation, no collisions after done)
    event.go        TranscriptEvent, ActionRequest, ActionResult, DisplayCard, (de)serialization
  internal/serve/
    hub.go          Hub: fan-out session.Update -> N subscribers, drop-oldest, finalized dedup
    dispatcher.go   Dispatcher: route ActionRequest verbs (speak/display/session.*/agent.result)
    tts.go          lazy Synthesizer + PlayFloat32 + mic-mute barge-in guard
    transport.go    Transport interface (Publish/Actions/lifecycle)
    ws.go           WebSocket transport (Track A)
    grpc.go         gRPC transport (Track A)
    serve.proto     gRPC service + messages (Track A)
    agent.go        AgentHandler interface + ExternalAgent (Track B)
    gemini.go       GeminiAgent (google.golang.org/genai function-calling) (Track B)
    intent.go       fast-path regex/rules intent matcher (Track B)
    retriever.go    Retriever interface for RAG/lookup tool (Track B)
  cmd/moonshine/serve.go   serveCmd wiring (Phase 5, after both tracks land)

Each file is owned by exactly one task to keep two agents in separate files. Shared types (event package) are frozen in Phase 1 before either track starts.
acceptance_criteria:
- 'moonshine serve' starts, opens mic, and streams TranscriptEvent JSON to connected WS and gRPC subscribers with interim + finalized lines.
- A subscriber can POST an action (speak/display/session control) and observe the effect (TTS plays; display card fanned out; session pauses).
- Built-in Gemini agent, when enabled, turns a finalized utterance into a function call that maps to an action (lookup/display/speak) and speaks/display the result.
- Fast-path intent matcher handles at least one deterministic voice command without calling Gemini.
- Slow-subscriber backpressure drops interim frames but never loses a finalized line (idempotent on Line.ID).
- mic is muted during TTS playback (no self-transcription).
- --allow-actions gates speak/session-control/run_command; run_command off by default.
- 'make test' passes with no native libs (all new logic covered by fakes).

title: samples/browser-listen -- browser-as-audio-source demo (HTML+JS, no build step)
description:
Static HTML+JS page: getUserMedia + AudioWorklet captures mic audio in-browser, resamples/encodes to the PCM format serve.RemoteAudioSource expects (see pkg/serveapi/remote_audio.go's AudioFormat/AudioEncoding), streams it as binary WS frames to a running 'moonshine serve' instance, and renders the live transcript coming back over the same WS connection (reusing the same {kind:transcript,payload} decode pattern as samples/python-listen). No build step, no npm, no framework -- open index.html, enter a serve URL, click 'start'.

Demonstrates: the 'composability' pillar taken to its logical extreme (a browser tab is a full client, no install of anything), and directly exercises the Hostable Cascade epic's core browser-audio-source use case (docs/hosting.md).

Blocked on moonshine-go-cl3 (server-side --audio-source remote flag) -- cannot be built end-to-end against the real CLI until that lands.
close_reason:
Built samples/browser-listen: index.html + app.js + worklet.js. AudioWorklet converts mic Float32 blocks to int16 little-endian PCM, sent as WS binary frames; app.js decodes inbound {kind:transcript} JSON text frames using the same envelope every other sample uses. No build step, no framework, no dependency of any kind.

Verified the exact wire protocol end-to-end (not just read from source): built a throwaway Go harness that reads a real 16kHz WAV (moonshine's own test-assets/two_cities_16k.wav) and streams it as int16 LE PCM binary WS frames in 20ms chunks against a real 'moonshine serve --audio-source remote --remote-audio-encoding int16 --remote-audio-rate 16000' process -- got back correct, accurate real-speech transcription of 'A Tale of Two Cities' over the WS connection, confirming the exact encoding/framing app.js/worklet.js implement actually works against the real --audio-source remote code path (cl3). Browser automation wasn't available to click through getUserMedia/AudioWorklet directly, but the wire protocol -- the actual risk in a browser-facing sample -- was verified with real audio and a real server, not assumed from reading pkg/serveapi/remote_audio.go's decodePCM alone.

title: moonshine serve: wire RemoteAudioSource into a CLI flag (e.g. --audio-source remote)
description:
internal/serve/ws.go's WSTransport.SetAudioSink(*RemoteAudioSource) and the binary-frame handling it needs already exist (moonshine-go-elj, tested via TestWSTransport_BinaryAudioStreaming), but cmd/moonshine/serve.go never calls SetAudioSink -- there is currently no way to start 'moonshine serve' with a RemoteAudioSource instead of the local mic. This capability is only reachable today by writing Go code against internal/serve.Server directly (in-module), not via the shipped CLI.

Found while scoping a browser-as-audio-source sample (see docs/hosting.md's 'Browser as the audio source' subsection, and the Hostable Cascade epic moonshine-go-f26/elj) -- the sample can't be built end-to-end against the real CLI without this.

Requested shape: a flag on 'moonshine serve' (e.g. --audio-source remote, alongside the existing local/mic default) that swaps AudioSource for a serve.NewRemoteAudioSource(...) and calls WSTransport.SetAudioSink on it instead of opening the mic. Also note: gRPC transport (internal/serve/grpc.go) has no equivalent SetAudioSink wiring at all -- WS-only for now; scope this ticket to WS unless gRPC support is trivial to add alongside.
close_reason: Added --audio-source (local|remote), --remote-audio-rate, --remote-audio-encoding, and --remote-audio-channels flags to serveCmd in cmd/moonshine/serve.go. Wired remote mode to construct serve.NewRemoteAudioSource and attach SetAudioSink on WSTransport. Unit-tested.

One new minor bug filed along the way: moonshine-go-b6f (TranscriptEvent.Lines JSON null on empty, P3, not blocking).

title: samples/cascade-faq -- Tier 1/2 flagship: offline voice FAQ agent via pkg/serveapi
description: Own Go module under samples/cascade-faq with a 'replace github.com/ghchinoy/moonshine-go => ../..' directive (simulating a real external consumer depending on the published module, while still testable against local HEAD). Depends only on pkg/serveapi (+ nhooyr.io/websocket for the transport dial). Connects to a running 'moonshine serve --allow-actions' over WS, decodes TranscriptEvent payloads into serveapi.TranscriptEvent, drives a local serveapi.AgentRunner wired with serveapi.NewCompositeHandler(intentMatcher, faqHandler) where faqHandler wraps a serveapi.NewStaticRetriever seeded with docs/MISSION.md-derived Q&A content, and an ActionSink that sends resulting ActionRequest{Verb:speak} JSON frames back over the same WS connection. Fully offline, no LLM API key, no genai dependency -- demonstrates the composability + privacy + control pillars from docs/MISSION.md end-to-end against the REAL running daemon, and is the first real external consumer exercising pkg/serveapi's wire-compatibility (should surface any further drift between serveapi and internal/serve/event types). Includes a README.md walkthrough (start moonshine serve, run the sample, ask it something, hear it answer).
close_reason:
Built samples/cascade-faq: own Go module (replace directive to local checkout, simulating a real external consumer), depends only on pkg/serveapi + nhooyr.io/websocket, builds clean with CGO_ENABLED=0. Implements a full external Tier 1 agent: serveapi.AgentRunner + serveapi.CompositeHandler(controlHandler, faqHandler), a wsActionSink correlating ActionRequest/ActionResult over the shared WS connection, a fast-path regex controlHandler (session.pause/resume, mirroring internal/serve's IntentMatcher pattern independently), and a faqHandler genuinely calling serveapi.StaticRetriever.Retrieve for answers sourced from docs/MISSION.md, speaking them back via ActionRequest{Verb:speak}.

Verified fully live end-to-end against a real running 'moonshine serve --allow-actions' daemon (real mic, real libmoonshine, real TTS) -- not mocked: spoke a sentence containing 'privacy', confirmed [you said]/[agent] log lines, confirmed the retrieved MISSION.md snippet was spoken back with no daemon-side error.

Found and fixed a real P1 bug in the process (moonshine-go-ule): cmd/moonshine/serve.go never passed g2p_root to the TTS synthesizer, so every speak action failed in the running daemon even in an environment where 'moonshine tts' worked fine. Fixed to mirror cmd/moonshine/tts.go's existing g2p_root wiring (3-line change, cmd/moonshine/serve.go only, no overlap with 5ng/8uk's file scope) -- verified fix live (speak succeeded after the fix, failed before it) and go build/vet/test/gofmt clean across root + both sample modules.

Also surfaced moonshine-go-6ba (raw PCM in every transcript frame) live via this sample (documented client-side workaround, same as samples/listen).

title: samples/listen -- Tier 0 minimal WS transcript client
description: Own Go module under samples/listen (separate go.mod, module path samples/listen or similar -- NOT under github.com/ghchinoy/moonshine-go so Go structurally cannot import internal/*). Dials moonshine serve's ws://.../ws endpoint directly with nhooyr.io/websocket (or stdlib-adjacent minimal dep), decodes the {kind, payload} wire envelope by hand (no serveapi dependency -- this sample proves the wire format itself is the contract, usable from literally any language), dedups on finalized_line_ids, prints '[FINAL] <text>' lines. ~50-80 lines. Includes a README.md showing how to run it against 'moonshine serve --transport ws'.

title: No importable entrypoint to run the sidecar daemon with a custom in-process agent (cmd/moonshine/serve.go is package main)
description:
Found while scoping samples/ (moonshine-go-igx) and confirmed by reading cmd/moonshine/serve.go directly.

The entire daemon assembly (loading the model, opening the mic, wiring Hub/Dispatcher/Transport Manager/AgentRunner, the TTS Speaker + barge-in mute hook, signal handling) lives in runServe() inside cmd/moonshine/serve.go, which is 'package main'. Go's language rule (not internal/ visibility) makes package main uninformable by any other package, including code in this same module.

Concretely this means: even after 8uk (internal/serve consumes pkg/serveapi) and 5ng (session.NewLive takes serveapi.AudioSource) land, there is still no way for a Go program -- in this module or outside it -- to call something like 'start the real sidecar daemon, but with my own serveapi.AgentHandler compiled in' without literally copy-pasting runServe's ~100 lines. Tier 2 as docs/quickstart-voice-agent.md currently describes it (implement AgentHandler, 'wire it with CompositeHandler+AgentRunner+dispatcher' and have it drive the real daemon) is not actually achievable via in-process embedding today -- only by running a separate process that connects over WS/gRPC as a Tier 1 external agent using serveapi types for its own internal wiring (still valuable, just a different integration shape than what Tier 2 implies).

Proposed fix (not implemented, just scoped): extract runServe's post-flag-parsing wiring into an importable function, e.g. internal/serve.Run(ctx, Config{..., Agent serveapi.AgentHandler, ...}) (or similar), with cmd/moonshine/serve.go reduced to flag parsing + calling it. This is a bigger, riskier refactor than 8uk/5ng (touches the one file every existing task carefully avoided per docs/serve-sidecar.md's file-ownership map) so it should be its own reviewed task, not folded silently into either.

Not blocking samples/cascade-faq: that sample is built as a genuine external Tier 1 process using pkg/serveapi types locally (dial WS, run AgentRunner+CompositeHandler client-side, send ActionRequests back) -- which is real and valuable, just not 'embed my agent in the actual daemon process' as Tier 2's docs currently promise. Flagging so the quickstart's Tier 2 section and this gap get reconciled together (relates to zj2/5j4).
close_reason: Extracted importable daemon Server / ServerConfig into internal/serve/server.go and refactored cmd/moonshine/serve.go to delegate to it.

title: pkg/serveapi.StaticRetriever diverges from internal/serve.StaticRetriever (Source field + empty-query behavior)
description:
Found while building samples/cascade-faq (moonshine-go-igx), which uses pkg/serveapi.StaticRetriever as a real external consumer would.

pkg/serveapi/retriever.go's StaticRetriever.Retrieve differs from the pre-existing, tested internal/serve/retriever.go's StaticRetriever.Retrieve (the type it's supposed to be a public on-ramp for) in two ways:

1. Empty query: pkg/serveapi returns ALL items (serveapi_test.go asserts this at ~line 117-120). internal/serve returns NO items (early 'if q == "" { return nil, nil }'). Opposite behavior for the same input.
2. Field coverage: pkg/serveapi only matches Title/Snippet. internal/serve also matches Source. A query that only matches an item's Source field finds it via internal/serve but not via pkg/serveapi.

Since 8uk plans to make internal/serve consume pkg/serveapi's types (single source of truth), this divergence needs to be resolved as part of that refactor, not left as two permanently-different behaviors. Recommend: pick internal/serve's original behavior (empty query -> no results; match Source too) since it already has real usage assumptions in gemini.go/tests, and update pkg/serveapi + its test to match, OR if the empty-query semantics is being intentionally changed as a public-API improvement, document it explicitly in retriever.go's doc comment (it currently reads as if it's just mirroring the internal type, which it doesn't).

Not blocking my samples work -- cascade-faq's demo dataset doesn't rely on Source-field matching or empty-query behavior -- but will silently reintroduce this bug if 8uk's mapper doesn't reconcile it.
close_reason: Fixed in 8uk: pkg/serveapi.StaticRetriever now matches internal/serve behavior (returns nil on empty query, matches Source field).

title: Epic: samples/ — DevRel showcase apps for the cascade
description:
As moonshine-go's DevRel engineer (not a CLI user), build small, compelling sample apps under samples/ that demonstrate the STT->LLM->TTS cascade's real advantages per docs/MISSION.md (control, observability, privacy, composability) and dogfood pkg/serveapi as a genuine third-party Go consumer would use it (separate Go module, module path that does NOT share the moonshine-go path element, so Go's internal/ visibility rule structurally blocks any accidental import of internal/*).

Two flagship samples:
1. samples/listen -- Tier 0 'hello world': ~50-line Go program, own module, dials moonshine serve's WebSocket endpoint, decodes the wire envelope, prints finalized transcript lines. Proves the transcript is a real cross-process bus in any language (here: minimal Go, no serveapi needed) -- the 'composability' pillar with zero ceremony.
2. samples/cascade-faq -- Tier 1/2 flagship: separate Go module depending on pkg/serveapi (via replace directive to the local checkout, matching how a real external consumer would vendor/require it once published). An external agent process that connects over WS, runs serveapi.AgentRunner + serveapi.CompositeHandler (fast-path intent matching + a serveapi.StaticRetriever grounded on docs/MISSION.md content) against live finalized lines, and speaks answers back via ActionRequest{Verb:speak}. Works fully offline/local, no LLM API key required -- demonstrates all four MISSION.md pillars in one small app, and dogfoods pkg/serveapi end-to-end against the real running daemon (the first real external consumer of that package).

Report any real defects/gaps found in pkg/serveapi or internal/serve back to the core moonshine-go agent as new bd tasks (discovered-from this epic), rather than silently working around them in the samples.
close_reason: Both flagship samples built and verified live end-to-end (real mic, real libmoonshine, real TTS -- not mocked): samples/listen (moonshine-go-i08, Tier 0) and samples/cascade-faq (moonshine-go-90h, Tier 1/2 flagship, first real external consumer of pkg/serveapi). Fixed one real bug found along the way (moonshine-go-ule, TTS g2p_root wiring). Two further real bugs filed for the core agent, not blocking: moonshine-go-6ba (P1, raw PCM bloats every transcript frame) and moonshine-go-2al (StaticRetriever divergence, to reconcile alongside 8uk). One scoping gap filed for later architectural discussion: moonshine-go-ied (no importable daemon entrypoint for true in-process Tier 2 embedding).

title: Document Apple 'container' as a local Linux/dlopen verification workflow
description:
docs: cover Apple's native 'container' CLI (macOS 15+/Sequoia+, ships with Xcode / via 'brew install container' or Apple's installer) as a Docker-Desktop-free way for contributors on Apple Silicon to verify Linux behavior locally -- specifically dlopen()-testing fetched libmoonshine release assets, and later prototyping the moonshine-go-d15 serve-in-a-box image.

This was validated for real: it's how the GLIBC 2.43 regression in the v0.0.71 linux-x86_64 release asset (moonshine-go-dh7) was actually discovered -- 'container run --arch amd64 ubuntu:22.04' + ctypes.CDLL() caught what the static RPATH/bundling check (scripts/check-release-asset.sh) could not.

Cover: installing/checking for 'container', running a native arm64 container vs. an emulated --arch amd64 one (with the tradeoffs -- native is fast, emulated is slower but catches x86_64-specific issues like dh7), the exact copy-in-and-dlopen-test recipe (container run -d, container exec to install curl/python3, container cp to stage a locally-fetched .moonshine/lib, then ctypes.CDLL() or a tiny Go program calling moonshine.Load()), and cleanup (container stop/rm).
design: New doc, most likely docs/testing-with-container.md (or a section in docs/RELEASING.md alongside the fetchlib/GLIBC verification workflow it directly supports -- fold in rather than creating a new top-level doc if RELEASING.md is the better fit once drafted). Link from docs/README.md's index. Cross-reference from moonshine-go-glg / moonshine-go-dh7's descriptions and from moonshine-go-d15 (serve-in-a-box) as a way to locally test that image before it has real CI coverage.
acceptance_criteria:
- A doc exists (or a scoped section in an existing doc) walking through installing 'container', running a Linux container (native + --arch amd64 emulated), and using it to dlopen()-test a fetched libmoonshine release asset.
- docs/README.md links to it.
- References the GLIBC 2.43 finding (moonshine-go-dh7) as the motivating real-world example.

title: pkg/serveapi: scaffold leaf package (interfaces + shadow types)
description: Create pkg/serveapi with the public interfaces and shadow structs: AgentHandler, CompositeHandler, AgentRunner, ExternalAgent, ActionSink/ActionSinkFunc; Retriever, Result, NoopRetriever, StaticRetriever; LLMClient, Turn, ToolCall, ToolResponse (interface + structs only, no genai); TranscriptEvent, ActionRequest, ActionResult, DisplayCard, SpeakArgs, Kind consts; Line, Word, SpeakerSpan; AudioSource. Stdlib-only imports (internal/moonshine allowed only if truly needed; prefer none). AudioSource must design for NETWORK producers (browser mic per elj), not just the local mic: interface = { Chunks() <-chan []float32; Err() error } where Err() (checked after Chunks() closes) distinguishes clean EOF from abnormal termination (dropped connection). Godoc must state implementations own their OWN ingest backpressure (bounded buffer/flow control) -- the Hub's drop-oldest fan-out pattern must NOT be applied to audio ingest (dropping audio corrupts the transcript). See docs/vision/serveapi-design.md.
design: Zero internal/session and internal/audio imports. Shadow structs mirror internal/serve/event + moonshine.Line JSON tags exactly so the wire format is unchanged. AudioSource: Chunks() <-chan []float32 + Err() error (bufio.Scanner.Err convention); document impl-owned backpressure. This session scaffolds pkg/serveapi ONLY; the internal/serve refactor to consume it is 8uk (separate review).
acceptance_criteria: Package compiles; CGO_ENABLED=0 go build ./pkg/serveapi/... passes; no import of internal/session or internal/audio; JSON tags match current wire format.
close_reason: Scaffolded pkg/serveapi: doc.go, line.go (Line/Word/SpeakerSpan shadow structs), event.go (TranscriptEvent+FinalizedLines helper, SessionSummary, ActionRequest/Result, DisplayCard, SpeakArgs, DisplayArgs, Kind consts), retriever.go (Retriever/Result/Noop/Static), llm.go (LLMClient/Turn/ToolCall/ToolResponse, SDK-decoupled), agent.go (AgentHandler/ExternalAgent/CompositeHandler/ActionSink(Func)/AgentRunner), audio.go (AudioSource with Chunks()+Err() and impl-owned-backpressure godoc). Gates: CGO_ENABLED=0 build PASS; stdlib-only imports (no internal/*); go vet clean; serveapi_test.go passes; existing internal/serve tests unaffected. JSON tags match internal wire format. internal/serve refactor is 8uk (not touched).

Extracted sourceClosedUpdate() helper so the Err()-after-close contract (clean EOF vs abnormal termination) is unit-testable without a native *moonshine.Stream; added fakeAudioSource + 2 tests. Change AgentHandler.OnFinalizedLine(moonshine.Line) -> serveapi.Line; add mappers moonshine.Line->serveapi.Line and session.Update->serveapi.TranscriptEvent (session/audio imports stay here); make gemini.go/intent.go/retriever.go implement the public interfaces. title: Epic: pkg/serveapi — public Go extension surface for Tier 2 agents
description: Add a public pkg/serveapi package so Go programs can implement AgentHandler/Retriever/LLMClient and link them in-process (Tier 2), instead of the currently-documented-but-broken 'import internal/serve' path (forbidden by Go internal/ rules for external modules). IPC/JSON (WS+gRPC) stays the primary, language-agnostic surface; pkg/serveapi is an ADDITIONAL Go-native on-ramp. Amends the locked decision in docs/serve-sidecar.md §2 ('no promotion to pkg/'). HARD CONSTRAINT: pkg/serveapi must build with CGO_ENABLED=0 — it must be a zero-internal-import leaf (stdlib + at most internal/moonshine, which is proven cgo-free; NEVER internal/session or internal/audio, which require cgo via malgo). Data/wire types are shadow structs (not type aliases) for stability. See docs/vision/serveapi-design.md for full rationale.
design: pkg/serveapi is a leaf package: interfaces (AgentHandler, CompositeHandler, AgentRunner, ExternalAgent, ActionSink; Retriever+impls; LLMClient interface only; AudioSource) + shadow structs (Line, Word, SpeakerSpan, TranscriptEvent, ActionRequest, ActionResult, DisplayCard, SpeakArgs, Kind consts). internal/serve becomes the consumer/source-of-truth-holder is inverted: pkg/serveapi holds the types, internal/serve imports them + holds conversion mappers (session.Update->TranscriptEvent) and concrete impls (GeminiAgent/genai, IntentMatcher, transports, Hub, Dispatcher, TTSSpeaker) that stay internal. 517 (swappable Transcriber) stays out — still design-stage.
acceptance_criteria: pkg/serveapi exists; CGO_ENABLED=0 go build ./pkg/serveapi/... passes (CI-enforced); imports never include internal/session or internal/audio; internal/serve consumes pkg/serveapi with no duplicated type defs and existing tests green; serve-sidecar.md §2 amended; quickstart Tier 2 imports pkg/serveapi.

title: TTS-to-bytes transport path + in-protocol barge-in
description: Replace local audio.PlayFloat32 playback with returning synthesized TTS audio over the transport for hosted use, and express barge-in as a protocol signal rather than mic.SetMutedFunc. Keep local PlayFloat32 path for the local-first default.
design: TTSSpeaker.Synthesize already returns audio; add a transport-return mode. Barge-in becomes a client/protocol event. Native-free tests with a fake speaker/transport.
acceptance_criteria: hosted mode returns TTS audio bytes to the requesting client; local mode still plays locally; barge-in works over the protocol; tests native-free.

title: Per-connection session manager + resource limits
description: Add a session manager so serve runs N independent sessions (each its own Stream + Hub + agent) instead of one process-wide broadcast session. Include lifecycle (create/teardown on connect/disconnect) and resource limits (max concurrent sessions, per-session memory awareness).
design: New entrypoint wiring in serve; factor runServe's single-session assembly into a per-session factory. Native-free tests for manager lifecycle with fakes.
acceptance_criteria: multiple clients get isolated sessions; disconnect tears down cleanly; a max-sessions limit is enforced; tests native-free.

title: Remote-PCM AudioSource: accept client audio over WS/gRPC
description: Implement an AudioSource that receives PCM frames from a connected client over the existing transports, so serve can transcribe remote audio with no local mic. Define the inbound audio wire framing (chunk size, sample rate negotiation).
design: Reuse WS/gRPC transports for an inbound audio stream; push decoded float32 chunks onto the AudioSource channel. Backpressure-aware. PRIMARY CLIENT = browser (getUserMedia + AudioWorklet), see docs/hosting.md 'Browser as the audio source'. Framing recommendation: raw little-endian PCM frames (Float32 or Int16) as WebSocket BINARY messages, sample rate agreed once at connect; transcript-out stays JSON text frames (wireEnvelope kind/payload). Sample-rate: browsers capture ~48kHz, Moonshine wants 16kHz mono (audio.TargetSampleRate/moonshine.SampleRate=16000); prefer client-side downsample in the AudioWorklet, else server-side (audio.Resample is pure-Go but currently lives in the cgo-tainted internal/audio pkg -- extract if used server-side). Opus/WebM via MediaRecorder is a bandwidth option but needs a server-side decoder. OPEN DESIGN POINT: ingest backpressure must NOT drop-oldest like transcript fan-out -- dropping inbound audio corrupts the transcript; use a bounded-buffer/flow strategy instead.
acceptance_criteria: a browser (or any client) can stream PCM to serve over a WebSocket binary channel and receive TranscriptEvents back on the same socket, with no local mic required; inbound audio framing + sample-rate negotiation documented; ingest backpressure strategy defined (not drop-oldest); native-free tests with a fake transport feeding PCM.
close_reason: Implemented RemoteAudioSource in pkg/serveapi and internal/serve with bounded buffer backpressure, Float32/Int16 PCM decoding, linear resampling, and WebSocket binary frame streaming integration. Fully unit-tested.

title: AudioSource interface: decouple session.NewLive from concrete mic
description: Introduce an AudioSource interface (Chunks() <-chan []float32 or equivalent) and refactor session.NewLive to accept it instead of the concrete *audio.MicCapture (session.go:136). Provide a mic-backed implementation preserving current behavior. Foundation for remote audio and file/stream transcription.
design: SUPERSEDED BY moonshine-go-5ng: the AudioSource interface now lives in pkg/serveapi (see docs/vision/serveapi-design.md + epic moonshine-go-y0k), not internal/audio/session. Original note: MicCapture already satisfies Chunks() <-chan []float32, so this is just changing session.NewLive's param type. Keep this task for the hosting-epic (f26) view; actual implementation tracked under 5ng.
acceptance_criteria: session.NewLive takes an AudioSource; mic path behaves identically; fake-source unit test passes; go test ./... native-free.
close_reason: Superseded by moonshine-go-5ng: AudioSource interface lives in pkg/serveapi and session.NewLive accepts serveapi.AudioSource.

title: Epic: Hostable cascade — run 'moonshine serve' beyond the local box
description: Enable hosting moonshine serve for remote clients (serve-in-a-box and bring-your-own-cloud), while keeping local-first as the default. The serve transport layer (Hub + WS/gRPC + JSON/proto) is already server-shaped and the STT engine is stateless PCM-in (Stream.AddAudio). Three edges assume a single local box and must be decoupled: (1) audio-in is hard-wired to audio.StartMicCapture and session.NewLive takes concrete *audio.MicCapture; (2) audio-out uses audio.PlayFloat32 (local device) + mic.SetMutedFunc barge-in; (3) one process = one mic = one broadcast Hub (no per-connection sessions). Deployment shapes: serve-in-a-box (container, demos/edge), BYO-cloud/self-host (recommended hosted shape, customer's VPC, privacy-preserving). Multi-tenant SaaS is intentionally left as an exercise for downstream users (not operated by this project, not architecturally forbidden). Thesis-preserving: audio stays where the operator puts it. See docs/hosting.md. Builds on the merged serve feature (epic moonshine-go-6nb).
design: One 'hostable cascade' core; deployment shapes differ by packaging/hardening only. Enabling refactors are edge-only; the model loop is untouched. AudioSource interface (mic | remote-PCM) also unblocks file/stream STT. Native-free tests via fake AudioSource where logic allows.
acceptance_criteria: moonshine serve can accept a remote client's audio (no local mic), return synthesized TTS audio over the transport, and run multiple independent sessions with resource limits; a container image runs it mic-less; BYO-cloud deploy artifacts + hosting docs exist; local-first default behavior is unchanged.

title: Surface confidence scores in TranscriptEvent for fast/slow routing
description: Lesson from online STT: confidence scores enable fast-path/slow-path routing (high confidence -> deterministic IntentMatcher; low confidence -> ask-to-repeat or escalate to LLM). Word.Confidence already exists in the binding (internal/moonshine/stt.go) and the C API (moonshine-c-api.h: 'Model confidence score, 0.0..1.0'). Surface per-word confidence (and/or an aggregated per-line score) through the serve event model (TranscriptEvent/Line) so agents and subscribers can route on it.
design: NO upstream caveat: confidence is already available at word level in Word.Confidence and in the C API. Work is: (a) ensure it flows into TranscriptEvent, (b) optionally compute a line-level aggregate (min/mean of word confidences) since Line has no confidence field, (c) document routing patterns. Frozen event types (serve-sidecar.md §8) may need a field add — coordinate if P1 already landed.
acceptance_criteria: TranscriptEvent carries word-level confidence (and optionally a line-level aggregate); a documented fast/slow routing example; native-free tests; no change to air-gapped guarantees.

title: Tunable endpointing/finalization policy for serve sessions
description: Lesson from Deepgram: a voice agent's responsiveness is dominated by ENDPOINTING (when an utterance is declared final), not raw WER. Today finalization is driven by moonshine's internal ~200ms re-analysis window and its built-in VAD (session.go trackLines / FinalizedLines). Add a configurable finalization policy in the session/serve layer: silence-duration threshold, min/max utterance length, and how long to wait after IsComplete before triggering the agent. This makes agent trigger latency a product knob instead of an implementation detail.
design: PARTIAL upstream caveat: moonshine-c-api.h exposes internal VAD with guarantees (lines never removed) but NO tunable endpointing/silence-threshold option in its recognized-options list. Basic policy (debounce/min-max utterance length, post-final delay) is implementable purely in moonshine-go's session/serve layer without upstream changes. Deeper VAD threshold tuning would require upstream libmoonshine support — VERIFY before promising that tier.
acceptance_criteria: serve exposes at least a basic, configurable finalization/endpointing policy (silence/debounce + min/max utterance) in the session layer; documented; native-free unit tests with a fake update source; upstream-VAD-tuning tier explicitly scoped as separate/blocked-on-upstream.

title: Docs: stt-landscape.md — what other cascade players (Deepgram/ElevenLabs/Whisper) teach us
description: Standalone landscape doc (docs/stt-landscape.md): what the online/other STT cascade-revival players do well and what moonshine-go's cascade can learn, even though they're cloud and we're local-first. Framing: 'local is a deployment property, not a capability'. Covers Deepgram (endpointing/VAD as tunable, keyterm boosting, formatting layers), ElevenLabs Scribe v2 (diarization/speaker labeling, word-timestamps + audio-event tagging, agent-positioned realtime), Whisper (multilingual/noise-robustness yardstick; chunk-batch not streaming-native = Moonshine's structural edge). Cross-cutting lessons: endpointing-is-the-product, structured transcripts, domain-vocab injection, confidence-driven fast/slow routing, hybrid end-state. Where online STT can't follow us (raw-audio egress, per-minute cost, can't co-locate STT+LLM+TTS). Hybrid future: swappable Transcriber backend framed as deliberate-but-uncommitted option mirroring swappable LLMClient, air-gapped-safe. Links each actionable lesson to its bd task. Companion to opportunities.md §7 (vs Live models) and serve-sidecar.md.
design: Prose-only, no build impact. All capability claims verified against internal/moonshine/stt.go and the moonshine-c-api.h header: Word.Confidence + model confidence score EXIST (no caveat); identify_speakers/word_timestamps/diarization_* options EXIST; keyterm/keyword/vocab boosting does NOT exist upstream (genuine caveat); no tunable endpointing/VAD option upstream (basic finalization policy implementable in session layer, deeper VAD needs upstream). Competitor claims stated factually. No invented moonshine flags.
acceptance_criteria: docs/stt-landscape.md exists; per-vendor strengths (Deepgram/ElevenLabs/Whisper) + cross-cutting lessons + 'where they can't follow' + hybrid-future section; each actionable lesson links its bd task; caveats accurate to the C API header; cross-linked from docs/README.md, README.md, and opportunities.md.
close_reason: Wrote docs/stt-landscape.md: framing (local is a deployment property not a capability); per-vendor strengths (Deepgram endpointing/keyterm/formatting, ElevenLabs diarization/word-timestamps/audio-events, Whisper multilingual+chunk-batch-vs-streaming-native edge); 5 cross-cutting lessons; header-verified caveats (confidence available now, diarization tunable now, keyterm NOT upstream, endpointing partly ours); where online STT can't follow (egress/cost/co-location); hybrid swappable-Transcriber future. Each lesson links its bd task (qii/kpd/7kr/517). Cross-linked from docs/README.md, README.md, opportunities.md §7.

title: Docs: quickstart-voice-agent.md — build-your-first-voice-agent tutorial (Tier 0/1/2)
description: Developer-facing tutorial (docs/quickstart-voice-agent.md): 'Build your first voice agent against moonshine serve'. Walks the same four-flagship progression as three on-ramps. Tier 0 (subscribe, any language): connect WS, print TranscriptEvent JSON, honor idempotency contract (dedup on Line.ID+IsComplete, never assume every frame). Tier 1 (external agent, any language): subscribe to finalized lines, POST ActionRequest (speak/display/session.*); build a deterministic command then a lookup that speaks an answer back. Tier 2 (in-process Go): implement AgentHandler/Retriever/LLMClient, use CompositeHandler to shortcut LLM via IntentMatcher, show LLMClient swap = local/offline deployment mode. Each tier ends with real 'moonshine serve' flags (--transport ws,grpc, --allow-actions, reused --arch/--language/--providers) and the frozen contracts it depends on. Relates to but does not duplicate P7 (.11, README/AGENTS).

title: P5d [Track B]: Retriever interface for lookup/RAG tool (retriever.go)
description:
TRACK B. Owns internal/serve/retriever.go ONLY.

title: P5a [Track B]: AgentHandler interface + ExternalAgent (agent.go)
description:
TRACK B. Owns internal/serve/agent.go ONLY.

Build:
- type AgentHandler interface { OnFinalizedLine(ctx, moonshine.Line) []event.ActionRequest }
- ExternalAgent: no built-in logic — returns nil (subscribers own all logic via IPC). This is the default so the daemon is a pure event/action relay.
- Agent runner: consumes Hub events (or a finalized-line channel), and for each newly-finalized line (dedup on Line.ID) calls the configured AgentHandler and forwards returned ActionRequests to the Dispatcher (P2). Optional end-of-turn silence debounce so the agent acts on complete utterances.

TESTS: agent_test.go — feed fake finalized lines, assert ExternalAgent produces no actions and that a fake handler's actions reach a fake dispatcher exactly once per Line.ID.
design: Depends on P1 event types + P2 Dispatcher interface. Isolated to agent.go. GeminiAgent (P5b) and intent matcher (P5c) are separate files implementing/feeding this interface.
acceptance_criteria:
- AgentHandler interface + ExternalAgent + runner defined.
- Finalized-line dedup + forward-to-dispatcher unit tested with fakes.
- No native libs.
close_reason: Implemented AgentHandler interface, ExternalAgent, CompositeHandler, and AgentRunner with deduplication and unit tests.

title: P5c [Track B]: fast-path intent matcher (intent.go)
description:
TRACK B. Owns internal/serve/intent.go ONLY. Deterministic voice-command fast path that runs BEFORE Gemini (cheap, offline).

Build:
- IntentMatcher: regex/rule table mapping finalized utterances to event.ActionRequest (e.g. /^(stop|pause) listening$/ -> session.pause; /^say (.+)$/ -> speak{text:...}). Config-loadable rules.
- Implements AgentHandler (from P5a) so it slots into the runner; returns actions on match, nil on miss (falls through to Gemini in the composite handler).

NOTE: This is the lightweight fast path. A future richer version can use moonshine's embedding-based Intent Recognizer — see linked issue moonshine-go-tpg (Intent Recognizer bindings). Do NOT block on that; regex/rules only here.

TESTS: intent_test.go — table of utterances -> expected actions / no-match.
design: Pure Go, no native deps, no network. Implements AgentHandler. Composable with GeminiAgent via a CompositeHandler (define in agent.go P5a or here — coordinate: put CompositeHandler in agent.go to keep intent.go single-purpose).
acceptance_criteria:
- IntentMatcher implements AgentHandler; rule table + at least one deterministic command tested.
- No native libs / no network in tests.
close_reason: Implemented fast-path IntentMatcher with default voice command rules and unit tests.

title: Implement audio playback using malgo in internal/audio
description:
Add a --play flag to 'moonshine tts' that plays the synthesized audio through the default output device immediately after synthesis, in addition to (not instead of) writing the -o WAV file. Needed a genuinely cross-platform playback path since this is a CLI tool used on macOS/Linux/Windows.

Solved via the existing github.com/gen2brain/malgo dependency (already used for internal/audio/mic.go's mic capture) -- miniaudio, which malgo wraps, supports a Playback DeviceType alongside the Capture type already in use, backed by CoreAudio (macOS) / WASAPI (Windows) / ALSA+PulseAudio (Linux) under one API. No new dependency, no new cgo requirement (mic capture already required cgo).
design:
internal/audio/playback.go (new): PlayFloat32(samples []float32, sampleRate int32) error, mirroring MicCapture's device setup but with malgo.Playback + a Data callback that copies from the samples slice into miniaudio's output buffer (zero-filling once exhausted). Blocks by sleeping for the clip's real duration plus a fixed 200ms drain margin, since miniaudio's data callback only signals when audio has been queued, not when it's finished actually playing out through the device's own buffering.

cmd/moonshine/tts.go: added a --play bool flag (viper-bound like the other tts.* flags); runTTS calls audio.PlayFloat32(out.Samples, out.SampleRate) after the existing SaveWAV call when set.

docs/user-guide.md: added --play to the tts flag table and an example.
acceptance_criteria:
- 'moonshine tts --play "..."' plays synthesized audio through the default output device and still writes the WAV file to -o/default out.wav
- Cross-platform via malgo/miniaudio (CoreAudio/WASAPI/ALSA+PulseAudio) -- no per-OS special-casing needed
- Manually verified on macOS: correct wall-clock timing (clip duration + ~200ms drain + device init overhead) and no errors

title: Add live.arch config key; doctor checks both transcribe/live archs
description: 'live --arch' currently has no config-key backing at all -- runLive calls modelArchFromFlag(liveArch) directly instead of going through flagOrConfig, so unlike stt.arch (shared by setup/transcribe) there's no way to persist a preferred live arch via config.yaml/env. This was intentional as far as NOT sharing stt.arch (streaming needs a different default), but the lack of any persisted key at all looks like a gap rather than a deliberate choice. Separately, 'moonshine doctor' checkModel only checks one (language, arch) pair (stt.arch's), so it could report [OK] on the STT model check while live's actual arch model is still missing.
design:
1. cmd/moonshine/live.go: runLive switches from modelArchFromFlag(liveArch) to flagOrConfig(cmd, "arch", "live.arch", liveArch) then modelArchFromFlag on the result. Update --arch flag help text to mention config key live.arch.
2. cmd/moonshine/root.go initViper: viper.SetDefault("live.arch", "tiny-streaming").
3. cmd/moonshine/config.go: add live.arch to configKeys table; tighten stt.arch's doc string now that live has its own real key instead of just a hardcoded flag default.
4. cmd/moonshine/doctor.go: extend checkModel (or add a second check) to check live.arch's model directory too, as a distinct row (e.g. 'STT model (transcribe)' / 'STT model (live)') only when live.arch != stt.arch (avoid a redundant duplicate row when they match).
5. setup itself is explicitly NOT changed (Option A, decided) -- it still resolves only stt.arch and downloads one (language,arch) pair per invocation; no auto-download-for-live-too behavior. Docs should make this two-invocation pattern explicit rather than implied.
6. Docs: README config table gets a live.arch row; a short explicit note near the Quickstart that setup has no knowledge of live.arch and must be run once per arch used; docs/user-guide.md's live section flag table + setup/config sections updated; docs/faq.md gets a short 'why do I need to run setup twice' Q&A.
acceptance_criteria:
- 'moonshine live' honors live.arch from config.yaml/env when --arch isn't explicitly passed, defaulting to tiny-streaming
- 'moonshine config list' shows live.arch with correct value/provenance
- 'moonshine doctor' reports separate rows for the transcribe-arch and live-arch model directories when they differ (and doesn't duplicate the row when they're the same)
- setup's own behavior is unchanged (still single (language,arch) per invocation, resolves stt.arch only)
- README/user-guide/faq updated per the design notes above
close_reason: Added live.arch config key (own default tiny-streaming, threaded through live.go's runLive via flagOrConfig same as stt.arch); root.go SetDefault, config.go configKeys entry + example; doctor.go's model check split into checkModels() reporting separate 'STT model (transcribe)'/'STT model (live)' rows (only when they differ). setup intentionally left unchanged (Option A). README/user-guide/faq updated to make the two-invocation setup pattern explicit. Verified end-to-end: config set/list, doctor's dual rows, live honoring live.arch by default and --arch overriding it, and the pre-existing (unrelated) base-streaming/en manifest gap reproduced identically via 'setup --arch base-streaming' confirming live.arch threads through the same code path as stt.arch.

title: Track partial-transcript stability stats (time-to-final, revisions) in live
description: Neither 'transcribe' nor 'live' currently track how long an interim line takes to stabilize/finalize, or how many times its text was revised while still partial. transcribe has no partial state at all (single batch call, every line arrives IsComplete). live does have partial states (interim lines revised as more audio arrives) but only tracks session-level TTFT/Elapsed/PollLatency and per-line LastLatencyMs (inference time) -- nothing aggregates per-line text-change history into a stability/flicker metric. The C API already exposes the raw ingredients per line (stable ID across polls, IsNew, IsUpdated, HasTextChanged, IsComplete) -- nothing downstream (internal/session, internal/tui/live.go, cmd/moonshine/live.go) currently accumulates them across updates.
design:
New types in internal/session/session.go:
  - LineTiming{ID, TimeToFinal, PollCount, Revisions, StabilityRatio} -- StabilityRatio = 1 - Revisions/PollCount (PollCount-normalized rather than a raw revision count, so a line seen 20x with 2 revisions doesn't look as unstable as one seen 3x with 2 revisions).
  - SessionSummary{LinesFinalized, AvgTimeToFinal, MaxTimeToFinal, AvgRevisions, MaxRevisions, AvgStabilityRatio} -- aggregates LineTiming across every line finalized in the session.
  Update gains: FinalizedLines []LineTiming (non-empty only on the poll where line(s) just completed) and Summary *SessionSummary (set only on the final Done update).

  Tracking logic lives in Live.Run's poll closure (session.go ~line 80-94): maintain an internal map[uint64]*lineProgress{firstSeen time.Time, pollCount, revisions int, lastText string, done bool} keyed by Line.ID. On each poll, for every line in the transcript: first sighting starts the clock (skip lines with empty text); while !IsComplete, increment pollCount every sighting and revisions when text changed since last sighting; the poll where IsComplete first becomes true computes TimeToFinal=time.Since(firstSeen), appends to Live's running []LineTiming (source for the end-of-session SessionSummary), and to this poll's Update.FinalizedLines. Attach the computed SessionSummary to the final Done update (session.go line ~101, after the last poll(moonshine.FlagForceUpdate) call).

  Note: Live.send already drops updates under backpressure (channel buffer 8, 'best effort... next tick carries a superset'). A dropped update could lose a FinalizedLines notification for live display, same accepted tradeoff the code already has for transcript updates -- doesn't affect SessionSummary since that's accumulated inside Live regardless of whether an Update was actually sent.

  Display:
  - TUI (internal/tui/live.go): extend statsLine()/liveModel to show the most recent FinalizedLines entry inline with the existing ttft/elapsed/last_poll footer (e.g. 'last_line: ttf=640ms revisions=2'), updated whenever one arrives. On Done, extend the footer with the full SessionSummary.
  - --no-tui plain mode (cmd/moonshine/live.go runLivePlain): the existing final stats: line (stderr) always gets SessionSummary fields added (low noise, one line). Per-line finalize notes are gated behind a new opt-in bool flag --line-stats (default false, stderr), to avoid changing today's --no-tui output format for anyone scripting/logging against it.

  Explicitly out of scope: live's --json mode doesn't exist for the transcript stream at all today (jsonOutput() there only suppresses status text) -- not building that as part of this. HaveSpeakersChanged firing on already-complete lines (diarization retroactively revising speaker assignments under --identify-speakers) is a related but distinct kind of post-finalization 'flicker' -- not counted in Revisions, which is pre-finalization text changes only.
acceptance_criteria:
- internal/session/session.go: LineTiming and SessionSummary types added; Update gains FinalizedLines []LineTiming and Summary *SessionSummary
- Live.Run tracks per-line firstSeen/pollCount/revisions via Line.ID across polls, computing TimeToFinal/StabilityRatio when a line finalizes
- SessionSummary is computed and attached to the final Done update, aggregating every finalized line in the session
- TUI (internal/tui/live.go) shows the latest per-line finalize stat in its stats footer during the session, and the full SessionSummary once Done
- --no-tui plain mode always prints the SessionSummary in its final stats: line; per-line finalize notes only print when a new --line-stats flag is passed
- Verified against a real multi-utterance live/streaming run (or the streaming smoke test) that TimeToFinal/Revisions/StabilityRatio are populated and plausible
- docs/user-guide.md's live section documents the new --line-stats flag and the summary stats
close_reason: Added LineTiming/SessionSummary to internal/session (Live.trackLines/summarize), threading FinalizedLines/Summary through Update. TUI shows last_line + summary in its stats footer; --no-tui gets an opt-in --line-stats flag for per-line stderr notes plus an always-on summary line. Unit-tested the tracking/aggregation logic directly (internal/session/session_test.go: revision counting, first-sighting finalization, empty-text skip, multi-line-per-poll, summarize aggregation) since live's mic capture can't be exercised headlessly in this sandbox (same limitation already documented for other live work). docs/user-guide.md updated.

title: Add 'moonshine doctor' subcommand for prerequisite/health checks
description: All prereq/troubleshooting knowledge today is passive documentation (README 'Prerequisites' section + docs/user-guide.md 'Troubleshooting'), reactive by design -- a user only learns something's missing when a command fails with an error string mid-run. There's no proactive check command. Add 'moonshine doctor' to consolidate this into one command, with OS-aware guidance (brew vs apt-style fix commands, etc.).
design:
New cmd/moonshine/doctor.go (cobra, --json-capable like other commands). Two tiers of checks:

1. Build-time prereqs (relevant before libmoonshine exists): cmake present + version, a C++20-capable compiler, git-lfs installed, a C toolchain for live's cgo build. Mirror scripts/build-libmoonshine.sh's own implicit requirements.
2.
